utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hi abigail how are you today

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hello hi nice to meet you i'm i'm doing okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: good i'm doctor sanchez and i'm gon na go ahead and take a look i saw with your notes that you've been having some knee pain yes that's that's true you know it's been going on for a while i like to run i do jogs i sign up for the 5k tack you know sometimes the marathon and i have n't been doing longer distances because

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: when i'm running i my right knee here it just starts to ache and it's it's just to the point where i need your opinion

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay what have you done for it so far what makes it better what makes it worse

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: well it used to be that when i run it ache and then i put ice on it and then it would be okay so i do ice and ibuprofen

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay and did you see anybody for this before coming into the office here

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i doctor wood is my primary care provider and i talked to him about it actually over the years and this last visit he said he referred me to you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay good so ice and rest makes it feel better running and and activity makes it hurt a little bit more is that correct

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that's right

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay do you have any family history of arthritis or any of those type of immune diseases

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm trying to think no i do n't think so no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and do you get is it is this primarily worse in the morning or does it is it just there all the time when it comes on

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it actually is worse towards the end of the day

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: once i'm on my feet all day it starts to ache towards the afternoon

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so let's go ahead and i want to do a quick examination here your blood pressure and was one twenty over sixty that's phenomenal your heart rate was fifty eight and you can tell that you're a runner with that that level of a heart rate and your respirations were fourteen so all of that looked very good there was no fever when you came in when i'm gon na just quickly listen to your heart and lungs okay those those sound good but let me get let's focus here on your lower extremities i'm i'm gon na look at your your left knee first when i move your left knee do you get any type of pain or is it just feel like normal and it's always your pain's always isolated to the right

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: that feels that feels normal

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay so let me i just want you to back up here in the stretcher a little bit more and i'm just gon na do some movement of your knee any okay so i want you to push your leg out against my hand does that hurt

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and if you pull back does that hurt a little bit

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and i'm gon na move it around so when i look at the knee there is no redness there's no swelling i can appreciate a a small amount of effusion and that means that there's a little bit of fluid under the knee or in that knee's joint space and there is there is several reasons that could be now when i push on your knee does it hurt more on the inside or does it hurt more on the outside here

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: the the right knee here hurts on the outside

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay okay and you've got a good pedal pulse so you know you can feel that and when i touch your feet you do n't have any numbness or tingling or anything like that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no uh uh

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay well so what i want to tell you is that i think you have a knee sprain from overuse and we see that sometimes in runners now unfortunately you're gon na have to take some a little bit of time off of of active running but i do n't think it will be that long until we can get you up and running again now i reviewed the x-rays that we did when you first came into the office here this morning and the joint spaces of that right knee are are well maintained i do n't see any evidence of any fracture and when compared to the left knee everything looks good so i do n't even see any signs of any arthritis that i would've been suspecting i would like you to stay on two tylenol five hundred milligrams and two ibuprofen two hundred milligram tablets and i want you to take that three times a day and that's gon na help with both the pain and the inflammation i'm also gon na order some physical therapy for your your right knee and that physical therapy will help strengthen the lower extremities and make it give you a little bit of a balance and some they'll be able to recommend good running exercises for you i do wan na follow up with you in two weeks and see if we're getting better so let's no running for two weeks and if we're we're improving then we'll move on and probably start adding some additional activity does that sound like a plan

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah that does i i was curious so i will lay off the running for now can i you know lift weights and do like my squats and and those type of exercises at the gym

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah absolutely and and those are good exercises but i'd like you to get that first physical therapy appointment in and they'll be able to talk with you on what the best exercises are for you to do

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay got it

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: any questions

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: hmmm no i do n't think so

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay thank you abigail and i'd like i said stop out at the desk and we'll make an appointment for two weeks

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay thanks doctor

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: thank you

detail_list.summary: The patient is an avid runner who participates in marathons.
Right knee pain.
The patient reports right knee pain during running.
The doctor asks about alleviating and aggravating factors for the knee pain.
The patient uses ice and ibuprofen to manage knee pain.
The doctor asks if the patient has seen anyone else for the knee pain.
The patient's primary care provider referred her for the knee pain.
The doctor confirms that ice and rest improve the pain while activity worsens it.
The doctor asks about family history of arthritis or immune diseases.
The patient denies family history of arthritis or immune disease.
The doctor asks about the timing of the knee pain.
The patient reports the knee pain is worse at the end of the day.
The patient's vitals are blood pressure 120/60 mmHg, heart rate 58 bpm, and respiration 14, with no fever.
The doctor performs an examination of the heart and lungs, which are normal.
The doctor performs strength testing on the right knee.
The patient reports no pain with strength testing.
The patient reports no pain with movement.
Physical exam of the right knee shows no erythema or edema, but a small effusion is present.
The doctor asks if the pain is on the inside or outside of the knee.
The patient reports pain on the lateral aspect of the right knee.
The doctor notes normal pedal pulses and no numbness or tingling.
The doctor diagnoses a right knee sprain due to overuse.
X-rays show well-maintained joint spaces with no fracture or arthritis.
The patient is instructed to take Tylenol 500 mg (2 tablets) and ibuprofen 200 mg (2 tablets) three times daily.
The doctor orders physical therapy and instructs the patient to avoid running for 2 weeks.
The patient will follow up in 2 weeks.
The patient asks if she can continue weight lifting and squats.
The doctor advises the patient to discuss specific exercises with the physical therapist.
The patient is scheduled for a follow-up appointment in 2 weeks.
